confidence.disclosure: Reconstructed primarily from Ibn Abd al-Zahir's Tashrif al-Ayyam wa al-Usur fi Sirat al-Malik al-Mansur — the court chancery secretary's official biography of Qalawun — which documents campaigns with day-level precision. Supplemented by Shafi' ibn Ali's continuation and al-Maqrizi's al-Suluk. The Homs victory (1281), siege of Marqab (1285), and fall of Tripoli (1289) are all dated to the day in Ibn Abd al-Zahir. Earlier Mamluk military service is less precisely dated.

sources: Ibn Abd al-Zahir, Tashrif al-Ayyam wa al-Usur fi Sirat al-Malik al-Mansur (c. 1291)
Linda Northrup, From Slave to Sultan: The Career of al-Mansur Qalawun (1998)

journey.event: Born a Qipchaq Turk in the steppes north of the Caspian around 1222.
Purchased as a mamluk by the Ayyubid sultan al-Salih Ayyub and enrolled in the Bahri regiment.
Fought in the Mamluk army that defeated the Mongols at the Battle of Ayn Jalut.
Returned to Cairo after the death of Baybars; subsequently became the power behind Baybars' young sons.
Enthroned as sultan in November 1279 after deposing Baybars' second son al-Adil Salamish.
Marched on Damascus to put down the revolt of the governor Sunqur al-Ashqar.
Received news of a massive Mongol invasion and marched north to Aleppo.
Defeated the Mongol army of Mengu Timur at the Second Battle of Homs on 29 October 1281.
Besieged and took the Hospitaller fortress of Marqab (Margat) in May 1285.
Captured the Crusader port of Latakia after an earthquake had damaged its fortifications.
Besieged and stormed the County of Tripoli on 27 April 1289, ending Frankish rule there after 180 years.
Returned to Damascus in triumph after the fall of Tripoli.
Returned to Cairo and began constructing his great funerary complex (madrasa, mausoleum and maristan) on Bayn al-Qasrayn.
Prepared a new army to attack Acre, the last major Crusader stronghold.
Died in Cairo on 10 November 1290 shortly after setting out for Acre; his son al-Ashraf Khalil carried out the final campaign.
